coredata.dc:title: Near-infrared carbon dots: pioneering emerging frontiers in biomedical applications

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials with sizes smaller than 10 nm, that are renowned for their exceptional properties, including superior antiphotobleaching, excellent biocompatibility, and minimal toxicity, which have received significant interest. Near-infrared (NIR) light has emerged as an ideal light source in the biological field due to its advantages of minimal scattering and absorption, long wavelength emission, increased tissue penetration, and reduced interference from biological backgrounds. CDs with efficient absorption and/or emission characteristics in the NIR spectrum have shown remarkable promise in the biomedical uses. This study provides a comprehensive overview of the preparation methods and wavelength modulation strategies for near-infrared CDs and reviews research progress in their use in the areas of biosensing, bioimaging, and therapy. It also discusses current challenges and clinical prospects, aimed at deepening our understanding of the subject and promoting further advances in this field.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: Â Â CiteÂ this:Â NewÂ CarbonÂ Materials,Â 2025,Â 40(1):Â 131-153 Â ReadÂ Online Review Near-infrared carbon dots: pioneering emerging frontiers in biomedical applications QianÂ HEâ€ ,Â ã€€Yan-liÂ YANGâ€ ,Â ã€€Rui-jiaoÂ LI,Â ã€€DanÂ MA,Â ã€€Li-yunÂ ZHANG* (Third Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, Shanxi Bethune Hospital, Shanxi Academy of Medical Sciences, Tongji Shanxi Hospital, Taiyuan 030032, China) Abstract: CarbonÂ dotsÂ (CDs)Â areÂ fluorescentÂ carbon-basedÂ nanomaterialsÂ withÂ sizesÂ smal- lerÂ thanÂ 10Â nm,Â thatÂ areÂ renownedÂ forÂ theirÂ exceptionalÂ properties, Â includingÂ superiorÂ anti- photobleaching,Â excellentÂ biocompatibility,Â andÂ minimalÂ toxicity,Â whichÂ haveÂ receivedÂ sig- nificantÂ interest.Â Near-infraredÂ (NIR)Â lightÂ hasÂ emergedÂ asÂ anÂ idealÂ lightÂ sourceÂ inÂ theÂ biolo- gical Â field Â due Â to Â its Â advantages Â of Â minimal Â scattering Â and Â absorption, Â long Â wavelength emission,Â increased Â tissue Â penetration, Â and Â reduced Â interference Â from Â biological Â back- grounds.Â CDsÂ withÂ efficientÂ absorptionÂ and/orÂ emissionÂ characteristicsÂ inÂ theÂ NIRÂ spectrum haveÂ shownÂ remarkableÂ promiseÂ inÂ theÂ biomedicalÂ uses.Â ThisÂ studyÂ providesÂ aÂ comprehens- iveÂ overviewÂ ofÂ theÂ preparationÂ methodsÂ andÂ wavelengthÂ modulationÂ strategiesÂ forÂ near-in- fraredÂ CDsÂ andÂ reviewsÂ researchÂ progressÂ inÂ theirÂ useÂ inÂ theÂ areasÂ ofÂ biosensing,Â bioimaging, andÂ therapy.Â ItÂ alsoÂ discussesÂ currentÂ challengesÂ andÂ clinicalÂ prospects,Â aimedÂ atÂ deepeningÂ ourÂ understandingÂ ofÂ theÂ subjectÂ and promotingÂ furtherÂ advancesÂ inÂ thisÂ field. Key words: Near-infraredÂ carbonÂ dotsï¼›Bioimagingï¼›Biosensingï¼›Therapyï¼›Biomedicine Â 1 Introduction Near-infraredÂ (NIR,Â includingÂ theÂ firstÂ NIRÂ win- dow Â 700â€“900 Â nm Â and Â the Â second Â window Â 1000â€“ 1700 Â nm) Â is Â regarded Â as Â an Â advanced Â diagnostic Â and therapeutic Â modality Â in Â nanomedicine, Â bioimaging, photothermalÂ therapy Â and Â other Â fields Â due Â to Â its Â ad- vantagesÂ of Â high Â resolution Â and Â deep Â tissue Â penetra- tion[1â€“3]. Â Although Â commercial Â dipyrromethene Â boron difluoride Â (BODIPY) Â and Â Alexa Â dyes Â have Â near-in- frared Â emission, Â their Â applicability Â is Â limited Â due Â to theirÂ highÂ costÂ andÂ complexÂ synthesis[4].Â OrganicÂ dyes are Â susceptible Â to Â photobleaching[5], Â and Â suffer Â from severe Â quenching Â effects Â caused Â by Â aggregation[6], which Â limits Â their Â potential Â for Â observing Â structural changes Â in Â live Â cell. Â To Â overcome Â these Â limitations, variousÂ nanomaterialsÂ basedÂ onÂ siliconÂ dioxide[7],Â rare earth Â materials[8], Â quantum Â dots[9],Â metals Â and Â semi- conductors[10â€“11]Â haveÂ beenÂ developed.Â TheseÂ nanoma- terialsÂ exhibit Â superiorÂ photostabilityÂ comparedÂ toÂ or- ganicÂ fluorophores. Â However, Â despite Â their Â advant- ages, Â the Â clinical Â translation Â of Â these Â nanomaterials facesÂ challenges. Â OneÂ major Â challenge Â is Â the Â require- ment Â for Â targeted Â ligands, Â which Â can Â complicate Â the synthesisÂ processÂ andÂ leadÂ toÂ nonspecificÂ interactions. Moreover, Â the Â complex Â synthesis Â routes Â associated with Â these Â nanomaterials Â can Â hinder Â their Â scalability andÂ practical Â application. Â Additionally, Â high Â cytotox- icityÂ observedÂ inÂ vitroÂ limitsÂ theirÂ suitabilityÂ forÂ clinic- alÂ use[12]. As Â a Â novel Â carbon-based Â nanomaterial, Â carbon dots Â (CDs) Â haveÂ uniqueÂ photophysical Â properties Â and goodÂ biocompatibility, Â thus Â exhibiting Â attractive Â ap- plication Â prospects Â in Â the Â field Â of Â biomedicine[13â€“15]. CDsÂ areÂ aÂ typeÂ ofÂ zero-dimensionalÂ (0D)Â carbonÂ nano- materialsÂ characterizedÂ byÂ theirÂ sp2/sp3Â carbonÂ frame- works Â and Â the Â presence Â of Â surface Â functional Â groups or Â polymer Â chains. Â In Â general, Â the Â synthesis Â of Â CDs canÂ beÂ achievedÂ throughÂ eitherÂ top-downÂ orÂ bottom-up approaches.Â Top-downÂ methodsÂ primarilyÂ involveÂ the exfoliationÂ ofÂ macroscopicÂ materialsÂ toÂ produceÂ nano- Â Â Â Received JulyÂ 20,Â 2024 Revised NovemberÂ 09,Â 2024 Accepted NovemberÂ 11,Â 2024 DOI: 10.1016/S1872-5805(25)60946-2 Â Â NEW CARBON MATERIALS Volume 40, Issue 1, Feb. 2025 Online English edition of the Chinese language journal CopyrightÂ©2025,Â InstituteÂ ofÂ CoalÂ Chemistry,Â ChineseÂ AcademyÂ ofÂ Sciences.Â PublishedÂ byÂ ElsevierÂ Limited.Â AllÂ rightsÂ reserved. sized Â carbon Â particles. Â This Â can Â be Â achieved Â through chemical Â oxidation, Â laser Â ablation, Â hydrothermal Â or electrochemical Â methods[16].Â Since Â the Â precursor Â ma- terials Â used Â in Â these Â top-down Â methods Â are Â typically crystalline, Â the Â resulting Â CDs Â exhibit Â mainly Â sp2Â hy- bridization[17]. Â OnÂ the Â other Â hand, Â bottom-upÂ prepara- tion Â methods Â are Â used Â to Â scale Â up Â the Â production Â of CDs.Â TheseÂ methodsÂ includeÂ traditionalÂ pyrolysis,Â mi- crowave-assisted Â methods, Â hydrothermal/solvotherm- al Â methods, Â magneto-thermal Â methods, Â microfluidic methods, Â and Â ultrasound Â methods[18â€“20].Â The Â influen- cing Â factors Â on Â the Â fluorescent Â properties Â of Â CDs mainly Â include Â quantum Â confinement Â effect, Â edge structureÂ effect, Â atomic Â doping Â effect, Â surface Â func- tionalÂ groupÂ effect,Â molecularÂ stateÂ effectÂ andÂ environ- mentalÂ effect[21â€“23].Â CDsÂ offerÂ distinctÂ advantagesÂ com- pared Â to Â other Â nanomaterials. Â Firstly, Â they Â exhibit variousÂ emission Â modes Â such Â as Â fluorescence, Â phos- phorescence,Â thermally Â activated Â delayed Â fluores- cence,Â andÂ chemiluminescence,Â makingÂ themÂ suitable for Â various Â types Â of Â bioimaging[24â€“27]. Â Secondly, Â CDs possessÂ good Â biocompatibility Â and Â photostability, Â en- ablingÂ real-timeÂ imagingÂ insideÂ biological Â systems[28]. Furthermore,Â CDs Â have Â tunable Â fluorescence Â emis- sion,Â andÂ theirÂ near-infraredÂ emissionÂ canÂ beÂ usedÂ for deep Â tissue Â imaging[29â€“30]. Â Some Â CDs Â also Â exhibit unique Â properties, Â such Â as Â the Â capability Â to Â cross Â the blood-brain Â barrier Â and Â target Â specific Â cells/subs- tance[31â€“34]. Â Lastly, Â CDs Â can Â be Â efficiently Â cleared Â by livingÂ organisms,Â makingÂ themÂ suitableÂ forÂ long-term bioimaging[35â€“36]. Â InÂ additionÂ toÂ servingÂ asÂ fluorescent imagingÂ agents, Â CDs Â are Â also Â utilized Â as Â nanoplat- formsÂ forÂ constructingÂ novelÂ compositeÂ nanomaterials orÂ directlyÂ usedÂ asÂ nanomedicines,Â dueÂ toÂ theirÂ unique structuresÂ orÂ targetingÂ properties[37â€“39]. Therefore,Â the Â development Â of Â CDs Â that Â can Â ab- sorbÂ orÂ emitÂ photonsÂ inÂ theÂ near-infraredÂ range,Â while being Â easy Â to Â synthesize Â and Â cost-effective, Â is Â highly desirable. Â However, Â NIR-CDsÂ often Â suffer Â from Â cer- tainÂ shortcomings,Â suchÂ asÂ lowÂ fluorescenceÂ quantum efficiency, Â wide Â half Â peak Â width Â and Â dependence Â on excitation Â wavelength. Â These Â limitations Â hinder Â their widespreadÂ applicationÂ inÂ theÂ biomedicalÂ field.Â Fortu- nately, Â with Â advancements Â in Â material Â systems Â and synthesisÂ methods,Â thereÂ haveÂ beenÂ rapidÂ progressesÂ in the Â development Â of Â CDs Â with Â improved Â absorption andÂ emission Â characteristics Â in Â the Â NIR Â region. Â Re- searchersÂ are Â actively Â exploringÂ newÂ strategies Â to Â en- hance Â the Â photophysical Â properties Â of Â NIR-CDs Â and unlockÂ theirÂ fullÂ potentialÂ forÂ biomedicalÂ applications. In Â the Â early Â stage, Â most Â CDs Â exhibited Â blue Â and greenÂ lightÂ emittingÂ areas,Â andÂ theirÂ penetrationÂ depth of Â tissue Â is Â only Â 1â€“2 Â mm[40],Â which Â significantly Â re- strictsÂ theÂ useÂ inÂ theÂ biomedicalÂ field.Â NIR-CDsÂ offer several Â advantages, Â including Â high Â tissue Â penetration, low Â interference Â of Â biological Â auto-fluorescence, Â and little Â damage Â to Â tissues[41]. Â Therefore, Â these Â materials areÂ suitable Â for Â deep Â fluorescence Â imaging Â of Â organ- isms,Â greatlyÂ expandingÂ theÂ applicationÂ ofÂ CDsÂ inÂ bio- medical Â imaging Â and Â other Â fields[42]. Â In Â addition Â to fluorescenceÂ imaging, Â certain Â CDs Â exhibit Â strong Â ab- sorptionÂ peaks Â in Â the Â NIR Â region, Â making Â them Â suit- ableÂ forÂ photoacousticÂ imaging[43].Â Meanwhile,Â theÂ di- verse Â functional Â groups Â present Â in Â CDs Â allowÂ for Â the coordinationÂ of Â paramagnetic Â metal Â ions Â to Â coordin- ateÂ withÂ CDsÂ forÂ magneticÂ resonanceÂ imagingÂ andÂ CT imaging[44].Â In Â addition Â to Â its Â excellent Â optical Â ima- ging Â diagnostic Â function, Â some Â NIR-CDs Â have Â been discoveredÂ recentlyÂ toÂ haveÂ excellentÂ therapeuticÂ func- tions[45]. Â NIR-CDs Â serve Â as Â effective Â photosensitizers forÂ facilitatingÂ photodynamicÂ orÂ photothermalÂ therapy. TraditionalÂ chemotherapy Â drugs Â can Â produce Â system- icÂ toxicityÂ whileÂ treatingÂ tumors,Â withÂ significantÂ tox- icÂ sideÂ effectsÂ inÂ vivo.Â PhotodynamicÂ orÂ photothermal therapyÂ canÂ confineÂ theÂ treatmentÂ toÂ theÂ tumorÂ siteÂ by preciselyÂ controlling Â the Â area Â of Â laser Â irradiation, Â al- lowing Â for Â targeted Â lesion Â therapy Â while Â minimizing harmÂ toÂ surroundingÂ healthyÂ tissues.Â InÂ addition,Â with the Â deepening Â of Â research, Â the Â NIR-CDs Â research Â on theÂ specificityÂ ofÂ theÂ lesionÂ siteÂ andÂ itsÂ microenviron- ment,Â andÂ theÂ targetedÂ therapyÂ hasÂ alsoÂ attractedÂ much attention[46]. ExistingÂ literature Â on Â CDs Â predominantly Â con- centrates Â onÂ short-wavelengthÂ emissionÂ spectra, Â there isÂ a Â notable Â scarcity Â of Â comprehensive Â analyses Â ad- dressing Â red Â and Â near-infrared Â emitting Â CDs. QianÂ HEÂ etÂ al:Â Near-infraredÂ carbonÂ dots:Â pioneeringÂ emergingÂ frontiersÂ inÂ biomedicalÂ applications Vol.Â 40Â |Â IssueÂ 1Â |Â Feb.Â 2025Â | 2 New Carbon Materials Moreover,Â current Â reviews Â tend Â to Â prioritize Â applica- tionsÂ related Â to Â cancer Â diagnosis Â andÂ treatment, Â over- looking Â the Â broader Â implications Â of Â NIR-CDs Â across otherÂ biomedicalÂ contexts.Â ThisÂ workÂ providesÂ aÂ thor- ough Â examination Â of Â the Â preparation Â methods Â for NIR-CDs Â and Â their Â diverse Â applications Â within Â the biological Â domain, Â encompassing Â sensing, Â imaging, andÂ therapeuticÂ interventionsÂ (SchemeÂ 1). Â WeÂ outline prospectiveÂ researchÂ directionsÂ andÂ theÂ potentialÂ clin- icalÂ translationÂ ofÂ NIR-CDs,Â emphasizingÂ theÂ needÂ for innovationÂ inÂ thisÂ rapidlyÂ advancingÂ field.Â OurÂ aimÂ is toÂ bridgeÂ thisÂ gapÂ byÂ offeringÂ anÂ in-depthÂ exploration ofÂ theÂ useÂ ofÂ NIR-CDsÂ inÂ theÂ biomedicalÂ field.Â WeÂ as- pire Â to Â inspire Â further Â research Â and Â development Â in thisÂ criticalÂ area,Â ultimatelyÂ advancingÂ theÂ understand- ing Â and Â utility Â of Â NIR-CDsÂ in Â various Â biological Â ap- plications.Â 2 Preparation of NIR-CDs Â 2.1 Guidelines for precursor selection According Â to Â literature Â reports, Â the Â selection Â of carbon Â precursors Â for Â commonly Â used Â near-infrared CDsÂ hasÂ aÂ considerableÂ influenceÂ onÂ theÂ morphology, chemical Â composition, Â andÂ optical Â properties Â of Â CDs. CommonÂ precursorsÂ includeÂ citricÂ acid,Â amines,Â phen- ols,Â polymers, Â fluorescent Â dyes, Â biomass Â and Â bio- molecules, Â and Â inorganic Â carbons Â (Table Â 1). Â Citric acid Â has Â abundant Â carboxyl Â andÂ hydroxyl Â groups Â and is Â often Â used Â to Â synthesize Â near-infrared Â CDs Â with ethylenediamine,Â urea, Â thiourea, Â silane Â coupling Â re- agentsÂ andÂ formamide.Â AmongÂ amineÂ compounds,Â re- searchÂ onÂ phenylenediamineÂ isÂ mostÂ prevalent[47].Â The benzene Â ring Â in Â aromatic Â amines Â offers Â built-inÂ con- jugation, Â while Â theÂ aminoÂ groupÂ providesÂ potential Â N dopingÂ and Â hydrophilic Â functional Â group Â at Â the Â peri- phery, Â enabling Â CDs Â to Â have Â higher Â quantum Â yield, long-wavelengthÂ emissionÂ andÂ desirableÂ waterÂ solubil- ity. Â Zhang Â et Â al.[48]Â prepared Â CDs Â using Â o-phenylene- diamineÂ asÂ theÂ carbonÂ precursorÂ andÂ underwentÂ amino protonation Â treatment Â to Â obtain Â CDsÂ that Â can Â achieve visibleÂ lightÂ waveÂ excitation,Â singleÂ photonÂ redÂ fluor- escenceÂ emission,Â NIRÂ lightÂ waveÂ excitation,Â andÂ two- photonÂ redÂ fluorescenceÂ emission.Â ProtonationÂ ofÂ 2,3- diaminophenazineÂ inÂ CDsÂ altersÂ theÂ molecularÂ config- uration, Â decreases Â the Â band Â gap, Â and Â ultimately Â leads to Â narrow Â peak Â width Â of Â red Â fluorescence Â emission. Aromatic Â amines Â are Â more Â suitable Â for Â synthesizing near-infrared Â CDs Â because Â the Â self-polymerization Â of aromaticÂ structures Â can Â create Â highly Â conjugated Â sys- tems Â with Â narrow Â bandgaps. Â Phenolic Â compounds, similarÂ toÂ aromaticÂ amines,Â easilyÂ adoptÂ coplanarÂ con- figurations,Â andÂ theÂ presenceÂ ofÂ hydroxylÂ groupsÂ intro- ducesÂ oxygenÂ defects,Â allowingÂ themÂ toÂ alsoÂ establish highly Â conjugated Â systems Â with Â narrow Â bandgaps. PolymersÂ possess Â distinct, Â repeatable, Â and Â control- lableÂ structuralÂ features,Â makingÂ themÂ suitableÂ forÂ the synthesis Â of Â CDs[49]. Â CDs Â formed Â from Â fluorescent dyesÂ as Â precursors Â can Â retain Â their Â original Â lumines- cent Â properties Â and Â solve Â the Â problem Â of Â poor Â water solubility[50â€“51]. Â Jiangâ€™s Â team[51]Â proposed Â the Â use Â of Cy7-CH3Â as Â a Â carbon Â source Â and Â hydrochloric Â acid dopamineÂ asÂ aÂ surfaceÂ passivatingÂ agentÂ toÂ synthesize near-infrared Â CDs Â emitting Â in Â the Â second Â near-in- frared Â window. Â The Â Ï€-conjugated Â system Â and Â rigid planar Â architecture Â of Â the Â carbon Â source Â can Â enhance the Â absorption Â and Â emission Â across Â a Â wider Â Heteroatom doping Size control Surface engineering Self-assembly Glucose TMB oxTMB Fluorescence PDT PTT O 2 OO O C O OH OH OH HO NH 2 NH 3 NH 3 H 2 N O 2 Â·âˆ’ Multi-enyzme acticity CDs H 2 O 2 H 2 O B io se n s in g Bioimaging T h e ra p y Emission tuning P re cu rs or s election SchemeÂ 1Â Â Â Â PreparationÂ ofÂ NIR-CDsÂ andÂ theirÂ applicationsÂ inÂ biomedical fields.Â TheÂ selectionÂ ofÂ precursorsÂ includesÂ citricÂ acid,Â ureaÂ andÂ p-phenyle- nediamine.Â StrategiesÂ forÂ modulatingÂ emissionÂ wavelengthsÂ involveÂ het- eroatomÂ doping,Â sizeÂ control,Â surfaceÂ engineering,Â andÂ self-assembly.Â Bio- sensingÂ canÂ beÂ achievedÂ throughÂ colorimetricÂ reactionsÂ inducedÂ byÂ the multi-enzymeÂ activityÂ ofÂ CDsÂ orÂ byÂ changesÂ inÂ fluorescenceÂ intensityÂ and wavelength.Â Additionally,Â theÂ excellentÂ fluorescentÂ emissionÂ propertiesÂ of CDsÂ enableÂ theirÂ useÂ inÂ bioimaging,Â whileÂ theirÂ photothermalÂ andÂ photody- namicÂ characteristicsÂ facilitateÂ diseaseÂ treatment QianÂ HEÂ etÂ al:Â Near-infraredÂ carbonÂ dots:Â pioneeringÂ emergingÂ frontiersÂ inÂ biomedicalÂ applications Vol.Â 40Â |Â IssueÂ 1Â |Â Feb.Â 2025Â | 3 New Carbon Materials wavelength Â range. Â Additionally, Â the Â presence Â of aminoÂ groupsÂ givesÂ theÂ CDsÂ aÂ positiveÂ zetaÂ potential, promotingÂ their Â interactionÂ withÂ bacteria. Â ThisÂ allows CDsÂ toÂ onlyÂ stainÂ theÂ cellÂ wallsÂ ofÂ drug-resistantÂ bac- teria, Â while Â bright Â fluorescence Â signals Â are Â observed throughoutÂ theÂ entireÂ cellÂ ofÂ normalÂ bacteria.Â Biomass andÂ biomolecules Â are Â widely Â available Â and Â inexpens- ive,Â makingÂ themÂ idealÂ precursorsÂ forÂ preparingÂ NIR- CDs.Â InÂ additionÂ toÂ commonÂ organicÂ precursors,Â some inorganic Â carbon Â materials Â (such Â as Â graphene Â and graphite)Â canÂ alsoÂ beÂ usedÂ toÂ prepareÂ NIR-CDs.Â 2.2 Strategies for NIR-CDs construction CDsÂ prepared Â fromÂ top Â to Â bottomÂ typically Â emit blueÂ light, Â while Â CDs Â with Â rich Â luminescent Â proper- ties Â can Â be Â obtained Â through Â bottom-up Â synthesis methods.Â TheÂ precursorsÂ chosenÂ forÂ synthesizingÂ CDs includeÂ bulk Â carbon Â material, Â small Â organic Â mo- lecules, Â aromatic Â compounds, Â and Â biomass[65â€“67]. Â The absorption Â spectrum Â and Â near-infrared Â emission Â can beÂ regulated Â through Â various Â methods, Â including Â het- eroatom Â doping[68], Â size Â control[69],Â surface Â engineer- ing[70], Â and Â self-assembly[71]Â (Scheme Â 2). Â Heteroatom dopingÂ involves Â introducing Â atoms Â of Â different Â ele- ments, Â such Â as Â nitrogen, Â sulfur, Â or Â phosphorus, Â into the Â structure Â of Â CDs[72â€“73].Â This Â modification Â can Â ef- fectivelyÂ tuneÂ theÂ energyÂ levelsÂ andÂ bandgap,Â thereby manipulating Â the Â absorption Â and Â emission Â properties inÂ theÂ near-infraredÂ range.Â ByÂ carefullyÂ controllingÂ the dopingÂ levelsÂ andÂ positions,Â theÂ opticalÂ characteristics ofÂ NIR-CDsÂ canÂ beÂ preciselyÂ modulated.Â SizeÂ control isÂ another Â crucial Â parameter Â in Â tailoring Â the Â absorp- tion Â and Â emission Â of Â NIR-CDs. Â The Â size Â of Â CDs Â can be Â fine-tuned Â during Â synthesis, Â leading Â to Â changes Â in theÂ energyÂ bandÂ structureÂ andÂ resultingÂ inÂ specificÂ ab- sorption Â and Â emission Â profiles. Â Smaller Â CDs Â tend Â to exhibit Â blue-shifted Â absorption Â and Â emission, Â while largerÂ CDsÂ preferentiallyÂ absorbÂ andÂ emitÂ inÂ theÂ near- infraredÂ region[74â€“75].Â InÂ additionÂ toÂ heteroatomÂ doping andÂ sizeÂ control,Â surfaceÂ engineeringÂ isÂ crucialÂ forÂ al- tering Â the Â optical Â properties Â of Â NIR-CDs[76â€“77]. Â By functionalizingÂ the Â surface Â of Â CDs Â with Â specific Â mo- leculesÂ orÂ ligands,Â theÂ surfaceÂ statesÂ andÂ energyÂ levels canÂ beÂ altered,Â influencingÂ theirÂ absorptionÂ andÂ emis- sion Â characteristics[78â€“79].Â Surface Â passivation Â tech- niquesÂ also Â enhance Â the Â quantumÂ yield Â andÂ photosta- bilityÂ ofÂ NIR-CDs,Â addressingÂ theÂ issuesÂ ofÂ lowÂ fluor- escence Â quantum Â efficiency Â and Â wide Â half Â peak width[80â€“81].Â Moreover, Â the Â chemical Â environment Â sur- roundingÂ theÂ CDsÂ canÂ beÂ tailoredÂ toÂ control Â their Â op- ticalÂ properties[82]. wavelengths[83â€“84].Â Defect Â en- gineeringÂ is Â an Â effective Â method Â for Â tuning Â the Â elec- tronic Â structure Â and Â dynamic Â behavior Â of Â the Â excited electrons Â in Â nanomaterials, Â leading Â to Â the Â emergence of Â novel Â optical Â properties. Â Qu Â et Â al.[85]Â reported Â a simple Â defect Â engineering Â method Â that Â introduced oxygen Â related Â defects Â with Â near-infrared Â bandgap emissionÂ whenÂ subjectedÂ toÂ post-processingÂ redÂ emit- ting Â CDs Â with Â 2-iodobenzoic Â acid Â (IBX). Â After Â IBX treatment,Â CDsÂ areÂ furtherÂ oxidized,Â withÂ someÂ nitro- gen Â atomsÂ replaced Â by Â oxygenÂ atoms, Â resulting Â in Â an increaseÂ inÂ oxygenÂ relatedÂ defectsÂ inÂ theÂ oxidizedÂ CDs (ox-CDs). Â Under Â 512 Â nm Â laser Â irradiation, Â it Â has Â a chargeÂ separationÂ rateÂ ofÂ 67.8%.Â UnderÂ whiteÂ LEDÂ il- lumination,Â theÂ photoÂ generatedÂ holesÂ inÂ ox-CDsÂ oxid- ize Â OHâˆ’Â to Â form Â Â·OH Â during Â the Â acidification Â of Â the aqueousÂ solution, Â while Â Â·OHÂ wasÂ not Â observedÂ under a Â 730 Â nm Â laser Â irradiation, Â indicating Â their Â potential forÂ non-invasiveÂ NIRÂ FLÂ imaging. QuÂ et Â al. Â haveÂ doneÂ aÂ series Â of Â workÂ inÂ theÂ syn- thesisÂ of Â red Â and Â NIR Â emission Â CDs, Â such Â as Â adjust- ing Â the Â proton Â characteristics Â of Â solvents[86],Â increas- ingÂ theÂ sizeÂ ofÂ CDs[87],Â introducingÂ electronÂ withdraw- ing Â groups Â on Â the Â surface Â of Â CDs[42], Â and Â preparing single/double Â layered Â graphite Â CDs[88]. Â In Â 2021, Â their group[89]Â developedÂ aÂ simpleÂ andÂ efficientÂ approachÂ to suppress Â the Â recombination Â of Â non-radiative Â charges throughÂ deprotonationÂ of Â theÂ surfaceÂ of Â CDs, Â thereby significantly Â enhancing Â the Â absorption Â and Â emission properties Â of Â CDsÂ in Â the Â NIRÂ region. Â ComparedÂ with the Â aqueous Â solution Â at Â room Â temperature, Â the Â NIR emissionÂ ofÂ CDsÂ inÂ N,Â N-dimethylformamideÂ andÂ gly- cerol Â increased Â by Â 50 Â times Â and Â 70 Â times Â atÂ âˆ’20 Â Â°C, respectively, Â owing Â to Â the Â improved Â deprotonation ability Â and Â increased Â viscosity Â (Fig. Â 1a). Â Recently, theirÂ team[86]Â selectedÂ aÂ moleculeÂ withÂ aÂ largerÂ conjug- ated Â domain, Â 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylicÂ dian- hydrideÂ (PTCDA),Â asÂ theÂ precursor,Â andÂ obtainedÂ CDs with Â the Â main Â absorption Â and Â emission Â peaks Â in Â the near-infrared Â band Â in Â aqueous Â solution. Â They Â further modified Â it Â with Â PEI Â to Â prevent Â the Â interaction between Â water Â molecules Â and Â the Â conjugated Â carbon nuclei, Â which Â in Â turn Â enhanced Â the Â NIR Â emission (Fig. Â 1b). Â Zhu Â et Â al.[90]Â developed Â a Â two-step Â bottom- up Â approach Â to Â synthesize Â NIR-carbonized Â polymer dots Â (NIR-CPDs) Â that Â exhibit Â bright Â emission Â in Â the NIR-IÂ andÂ NIR-â…¡Â transparentÂ imagingÂ windows.Â The strategy Â involves Â hydrothermal Â reactions Â to Â create stableÂ carbonÂ nuclei Â containing Â aldehyde Â groups, Â fol- lowedÂ byÂ KnoevenagelÂ reactionsÂ toÂ attachÂ theÂ molecu- larÂ emissionÂ centersÂ (Fig.Â 1c).Â NIR-CPDsÂ enableÂ high- performance Â NIR-â…¡Â angiography Â and Â facilitate Â real- timeÂ imagingÂ ofÂ non-invasiveÂ colitisÂ diseaseÂ severity. RoomÂ temperatureÂ afterglowÂ (RTA)Â materialsÂ are luminescentÂ substancesÂ thatÂ canÂ absorbÂ energyÂ atÂ am- bientÂ temperatureÂ andÂ emitÂ afterglowÂ evenÂ afterÂ theÂ ra- diation Â source Â is Â removed. Â Their Â prolonged Â emission helps Â eliminate Â background Â interference Â and Â light scattering,Â providingÂ significantÂ advantagesÂ forÂ applic- ations Â in Â areas Â such Â as Â biological Â imaging[91],Â espe- cially Â for Â multicolor Â afterglow Â CDs. Â When Â CDs Â are embedded Â in Â different Â matrices Â such Â as Â polymer, melamine,Â sodiumÂ chloride,Â aluminumÂ sulfate,Â zeolite, silica,Â urea Â and Â boric Â acid, Â the Â color Â range Â of Â after- glow Â emission Â has Â expanded Â from Â blue Â to Â red.[92â€“93] ZhuangÂ etÂ al.[94]Â usedÂ upÂ conversionÂ materialsÂ (NaYF4: Yb,Â Tm) Â to Â convert Â NIR Â light Â into Â ultraviolet Â or Â vis- ibleÂ light,Â andÂ activatedÂ theÂ afterglowÂ ofÂ CDsÂ basedÂ on RTA Â materials Â when Â excited Â by Â a Â continuous Â wave laserÂ atÂ 980Â nm.Â LiÂ etÂ al.[95]Â controlledÂ theÂ phosphores- cenceÂ colorÂ ofÂ CDsÂ fromÂ greenÂ toÂ nearÂ infraredÂ based onÂ theÂ aggregation-inducedÂ effect. TheÂ PETÂ systemÂ consistsÂ ofÂ aÂ signal-responsiveÂ unit,Â a connectingÂ bridge,Â andÂ aÂ recognitionÂ receptor,Â wherein theÂ fluorescence Â intensity Â is Â influenced Â by Â the Â elec- tronÂ transferÂ betweenÂ theÂ receptorÂ andÂ theÂ fluorophore. Specifically,Â ICTÂ refersÂ toÂ theÂ effectÂ ofÂ theÂ analyteÂ on the Â electron-donating Â (such Â as Â hydroxyl/amino) Â or electron-acceptingÂ (suchÂ asÂ nitro)Â capabilities,Â leading toÂ a Â blue Â shift Â or Â red Â shift Â in Â the Â fluorescence Â spec- trum.Â ACQÂ probesÂ areÂ primarilyÂ affectedÂ byÂ hydrogen bonding,Â hydrophobic Â interactions, Â electrostatic Â inter- actions Â and Â stacking Â effects. Â The Â surface Â functional groupsÂ of Â CDs Â are Â crucial Â for Â mediating Â the Â interac- tionsÂ amongÂ theseÂ mechanisms. FluorescentÂ sensorsÂ basedÂ onÂ CDsÂ possessÂ excel- lentÂ biocompatibility Â andÂ uniqueÂ optoelectronicÂ prop- erties.Â TheyÂ notÂ onlyÂ offerÂ diverseÂ structuresÂ andÂ rich tuningÂ capabilities,Â butÂ alsoÂ featureÂ abundantÂ andÂ cost- effectiveÂ precursors,Â eco-friendly,Â convenientÂ synthes- isÂ methods,Â outstandingÂ waterÂ solubility,Â lowÂ cytotox- icityÂ andÂ goodÂ biocompatibility.Â TheseÂ attributesÂ make CDs Â particularly Â well-suited Â for Â real-time Â detection andÂ imagingÂ ofÂ biological Â activityÂ markersÂ inÂ various organisms. Â Li Â et Â al.[97]Â proposed Â a Â fluorescent Â sensor basedÂ onÂ highlyÂ fluorescentÂ red-emittingÂ CDsÂ forÂ spe- cific Â real-time Â detection Â of Â polarity Â in Â solutions Â and livingÂ cells.Â TheÂ CDsÂ wereÂ synthesizedÂ fromÂ naphthol, citricÂ acidÂ andÂ methionine.Â NaphtholÂ playsÂ aÂ keyÂ role in Â expanding Â the Â Ï€-conjugated Â system Â of Â CDs Â to achieveÂ red Â emission, Â while Â citric Â acid Â and Â methion- ine Â are Â primarily Â responsible Â for Â introducing Â surface functionalÂ groupsÂ andÂ dopingÂ NÂ andÂ SÂ intoÂ theÂ carbon core Â to Â enhance Â fluorescence Â stability Â and Â quantum yield. NanoprobesÂ basedÂ onÂ NIR-CDsÂ haveÂ shownÂ sig- nificantÂ advancementsÂ inÂ bothÂ quantitativeÂ andÂ qualit- ative Â detection Â of Â target Â compounds[97â€“98].Â Chemilu- minescence Â imaging Â technology Â has Â the Â capability Â to simultaneouslyÂ and Â quantitatively Â describe Â and Â ana- lyze Â diverse Â factors, Â including Â the Â morphology Â and biochemistryÂ of Â diseased Â tissues. Â This Â renders Â it Â ap- propriate Â for Â early Â diagnosis Â and Â disease Â monitoring ofÂ tissueÂ lesions[99].Â ShanÂ etÂ al.[100]Â usedÂ citricÂ acidÂ and ureaÂ asÂ precursorsÂ toÂ changeÂ theÂ hydrophilicityÂ ofÂ ori- ginalÂ CDsÂ byÂ addingÂ octadecylamineÂ toÂ obtainÂ hydro- phobic Â CDs Â (M-CDs), Â and Â then Â combined Â M-CDs with Â bis(2,4,5-trichloro-6-carbopentoxyphenyl)Â oxal- ateÂ toÂ formÂ polymericÂ CDsÂ (P-CDs).Â BothÂ inÂ vivoÂ and inÂ vitro,Â thereÂ isÂ aÂ goodÂ signalÂ responseÂ toÂ H2O2,Â with aÂ linearÂ rangeÂ ofÂ 0â€“10âˆ’7Â mol/LÂ andÂ aÂ detectionÂ limitÂ as lowÂ asÂ 5Ã—10âˆ’9Â mol/L.Â CortisolÂ isÂ aÂ kindÂ ofÂ steroidÂ hor- moneÂ closely Â related Â to Â anxiety, Â depression, Â cardi- ovascular Â disease Â and Â other Â pathological Â conditions. EffectiveÂ andÂ rapidÂ methodsÂ forÂ measuringÂ cortisolÂ are crucialÂ forÂ evaluatingÂ andÂ understandingÂ humanÂ stress symptomsÂ andÂ healthÂ monitoring[101]. Â Li Â et Â al.[102]Â pre- pared Â CDs Â using Â reduced Â glutathione Â and Â formamide asÂ precursors,Â introducedÂ aptamersÂ (Apt)Â andÂ graphite oxide Â (GO) Â to Â build Â a Â cortisol Â sensor Â based Â on Â NIR- CQDs. Â NIR-CQDs Â Apt Â binds Â to Â the Â surface Â of Â GO throughÂ Ï€-Ï€Â stacking,Â resultingÂ inÂ anÂ internalÂ filtration effect Â (IFE) Â that Â leads Â to Â fluorescence Â quenching. WhenÂ cortisolÂ isÂ present,Â theÂ IFEÂ processÂ isÂ disrupted, causingÂ fluorescence Â to Â turn Â on. Â This Â sensor Â is Â cap- ableÂ of Â detecting Â cortisol Â under Â concentrations Â ran- gingÂ fromÂ 0.4Â toÂ 500Â nmol/L,Â withÂ aÂ detectionÂ lineÂ as lowÂ asÂ 0.13Â nmol/L.Â TanÂ etÂ al.[58]Â successfullyÂ synthes- ized Â NIR-CDs Â using Â magnolia Â leaves. The Â edge Â structures Â of Â CDs Â influence Â the Â electronic distribution,Â therebyÂ affectingÂ theirÂ luminescentÂ prop- erties. Â In Â CDs Â with Â zigzag Â edge Â structures, Â electrons areÂ concentratedÂ atÂ theÂ edgeÂ positions,Â leadingÂ toÂ aÂ re- ductionÂ inÂ theÂ energyÂ ofÂ theÂ conductionÂ bandÂ andÂ con- sequently Â causing Â an Â emission Â red Â shift. Â In Â contrast, localizedÂ electronsÂ inÂ grapheneÂ quantumÂ dotsÂ (GQDs) with Â armchair Â edge Â structures Â uniformly Â distributed across Â their Â surfaces. Â Doping Â with Â heteroatoms Â is Â a primary Â strategy Â for Â tuning Â the Â LUMO-HOMOÂ band gapÂ ofÂ CDs.Â Electron-richÂ atomsÂ (suchÂ asÂ N,Â O,Â PÂ and S) Â enhance Â the Â HOMO Â energy, Â while Â electron-defi- cient Â atomsÂ (like Â boron) Â decrease Â the Â LUMOÂ energy. SurfaceÂ functionalÂ groupsÂ significantlyÂ impactÂ theÂ lu- minescentÂ performance Â of Â CDs, Â with Â different Â func- tionalÂ groupsÂ generatingÂ multipleÂ uniqueÂ surfaceÂ emis- sion Â sites. Â Furthermore, Â the Â luminescent Â performance of Â CDs Â may Â arise Â from Â molecular Â state Â mechanisms. DuringÂ theÂ bottom-upÂ synthesisÂ process, Â theÂ lumines- cenceÂ intensityÂ inÂ CDsÂ isÂ influencedÂ byÂ theÂ molecular residuesÂ orÂ organicÂ luminescentÂ moietiesÂ present.Â 3.2.1Â Â Â Â One-photonÂ fluorescenceÂ imaging Compared Â with Â organic Â dyes, Â NIR-CDs Â exhibit excellentÂ cellÂ compatibility,Â adjustableÂ opticalÂ proper- ties,Â andÂ simpleÂ synthesisÂ methods,Â makingÂ themÂ suit- able Â for Â optical Â biological Â imaging[56]. Â The Â proton- atedÂ amino Â groups Â increased Â the Â Zeta Â potential, Â en- hanced Â the Â repulsive Â force Â between Â particles Â and Â led toÂ bulkÂ precipitateÂ dispersedÂ intoÂ nanoscaleÂ CDs.Â Ad- ditionally, Â the Â protonated Â amino Â group Â significantly changesÂ theÂ internalÂ bandÂ structureÂ ofÂ CDsÂ byÂ introdu- cingÂ numerousÂ n-orbitalÂ energyÂ levelsÂ withinÂ theÂ Ï€Â to Ï€*Â transitionÂ gaps.Â ThisÂ effectivelyÂ narrowsÂ theÂ trans- ition Â gap Â for Â photons, Â leading Â to Â single-photon Â NIR emissionÂ atÂ 622Â nmÂ andÂ 2Â photonÂ NIRÂ emissionÂ atÂ 629 nm. Nanoparticles Â that Â emit Â light Â in Â the Â red-NIRÂ re- gion Â under Â NIR Â excitation Â window Â (NIR-I, Â 650â€“ 950 Â nm; Â NIR-â…¡, Â 1100â€“1350 Â nm; Â NIR-â…¢, Â 1600â€“ 1870 Â nm; Â NIR-â…£, Â centered Â at Â 2200 Â nm) Â have Â been confirmedÂ to Â have Â potential Â applications Â in Â deep Â tis- sueÂ imaging, Â particularly Â in Â the Â NIR-â…¢Â range, Â which is Â oftenÂ referredÂ to Â as Â theÂ â€œgoldenÂ windowâ€�[119]. Â Shan et Â al.[120]Â firstly Â employed Â an Â in-situ Â solvent-freeÂ car- bonization Â method Â to Â prepare Â red/near-infraredÂ emis- sion Â CDs Â with Â a Â quantum Â efficiency Â of Â 57%. Â These CDsÂ wereÂ thenÂ utilizedÂ asÂ fluorescentÂ agentsÂ forÂ single photon Â and Â 2 Â photon Â imaging Â of Â mouse Â melanoma cells. Â Further Â study Â of Â the Â multi-photon Â fluorescence characteristics Â shows Â that Â the Â CDs Â achieve Â obvious fluorescence Â emission Â under Â the Â excitation Â of Â 800, 1200, Â 1600 Â and Â 2000 Â nm. Â Remarkably, Â this Â study achieved Â the Â first-ever Â demonstration Â of Â 3 Â photon fluorescenceÂ fromÂ CDsÂ excitedÂ byÂ theÂ thirdÂ biological window, Â as Â well Â as Â fluorescence Â emission Â from Â CDs excited Â by Â 4 Â photons. Â The Â ability Â to Â utilize Â different excitation Â wavelengths Â and Â achieve Â multi-photon fluorescenceÂ opensÂ upÂ newÂ possibilities Â for Â improved imagingÂ depth Â and Â resolution Â in Â biological Â and Â bio- medicalÂ research.Â 3.3 NIR-CDs for therapy Due Â to Â the Â abundant Â functional Â groups Â such Â as amino Â and Â carboxyl Â groups Â on Â the Â surface Â of Â CDs, drugsÂ canÂ beÂ covalentlyÂ loadedÂ ontoÂ CDsÂ orÂ incorpor- ated Â into Â drug Â delivery Â systems Â through Â Ï€-Ï€ Â stacking and Â electrostatic Â interactions. Â The Â constructed Â drug deliveryÂ systemsÂ canÂ effectivelyÂ regulateÂ theÂ distribu- tionÂ ofÂ drugsÂ inÂ vivoÂ andÂ reduceÂ sideÂ effects[121].Â InÂ ad- dition Â to Â drug Â delivery, Â CDs Â have Â made Â significant progressÂ asÂ nanomedicinesÂ inÂ theÂ fieldÂ ofÂ diseaseÂ ther- apy. Â CDs Â can Â be Â designed Â both Â as Â reactive Â oxygen speciesÂ (ROS) Â generators Â and Â as Â free Â radical Â scav- engers.Â EffectivelyÂ designingÂ CDsÂ withÂ ROS-generat- ing Â capabilities Â necessitates Â a Â deep Â understanding Â of the Â role Â played Â by Â surface Â oxygen Â functional Â groups. ResearchÂ has Â shownÂ that Â ketone Â groups Â serve Â as Â act- iveÂ sitesÂ forÂ theÂ decompositionÂ ofÂ H2O2Â andÂ theÂ gener- ation Â of Â hydroxyl Â radicals Â (â€•Cï¼�O), Â while Â hydroxyl groupsÂ actÂ asÂ substrateÂ bindingÂ sitesÂ (â€•Câ€•OH),Â and carboxyl Â groups Â function Â as Â inhibitory Â sites (â€•C(ï¼�O)â€•Oâ€•).Â NitrogenÂ dopingÂ isÂ alsoÂ utilizedÂ to enhanceÂ theÂ generationÂ ofÂ singletÂ oxygenÂ (1O2).Â InÂ ad- ditionÂ to Â nitrogen Â doping, Â incorporating Â highly Â elec- tronegative Â dopants Â (such Â as Â chlorine) Â into Â CDs Â can facilitateÂ energy Â transfer Â to Â oxygen, Â thereby Â increas- ing Â the Â activity Â of Â CDs Â as Â oxidants. Â Generally, Â the mechanisms Â by Â which Â CDs Â scavenge Â free Â radicals primarilyÂ involveÂ 3Â processes:Â hydrogenÂ transferÂ from surfaceÂ functional Â groups, Â electron Â transfer Â mechan- isms, Â andÂ theÂ formationÂ of Â freeÂ radical Â adducts Â at Â spÂ² carbon Â sites. Â The Â existence Â of Â sp2-richÂ domains Â en- hancesÂ theÂ formationÂ ofÂ adductsÂ withÂ freeÂ radicalÂ spe- cies. Â In Â the Â case Â of Â hydroxyl Â radicals, Â the Â electron- donatingÂ capacity Â becomes Â crucial Â due Â to Â the Â pres- ence Â of Â large Â sp2Â domains. Â Doping Â with Â electron- donatingÂ atomsÂ is Â a Â viableÂ strategy, Â but Â it Â is Â essential to Â remember Â that Â antioxidant Â activity Â arises Â from Â a complexÂ interplayÂ ofÂ multipleÂ factors. Â ForÂ theseÂ reas- ons, Â a Â clear Â distinction Â between Â graphene Â quantum dots Â (GQDs), Â carbon Â quantum Â dots Â (CQDs), Â carbon nanodotsÂ (CNDs),Â andÂ polymericÂ carbonÂ dotsÂ (PCDs) is Â fundamental Â to Â the Â design Â of Â suitable Â antioxidant materials[49]. Â Zhang Â et Â al.[122]Â developed Â CDs nanozymes Â derived Â from Â glutathione Â and Â biotin through Â a Â hydrothermal Â method. Â These Â CDs Â exhibit superoxide Â dismutase Â (SOD) Â enzyme Â activity Â along with Â red Â fluorescence, Â making Â them Â suitable Â for bioimagingÂ ofÂ intestinal Â inflammation. TheseÂ CDsÂ areÂ expectedÂ toÂ becomeÂ anÂ efficientÂ integ- rated Â nanomedicine Â for Â diagnosis Â and Â treatment Â of bacterialÂ infections.Â 3.3.3Â Â Â Â SonodynamicÂ therapyÂ (SDT) SonodynamicÂ therapyÂ (SDT)Â isÂ anÂ emergingÂ non- invasiveÂ treatmentÂ methodÂ thatÂ utilizesÂ low-frequency and Â low-intensityÂ ultrasound Â to Â stimulate Â the Â produc- tion Â of Â ROSÂ and Â eliminate Â tumor Â cells. Â This makes Â OCDs Â a Â promising Â candidate Â for Â multimodal combinationÂ therapy.Â 4 Conclusion and prospects CDs Â are Â promising Â carbon Â nanomaterials Â due Â to theirÂ lowÂ toxicity, Â abundant Â sources, Â easy Â functional- ization,Â good Â biocompatibility, Â excellent Â photostabil- ity,Â andÂ adjustableÂ fluorescenceÂ properties.Â TheyÂ have significant Â potential Â for Â application Â in Â various Â fields, includingÂ biomedicine,Â environmentalÂ protection,Â pho- tocatalysis, Â and Â energy Â storage Â and Â conversion. Â The historical Â development Â of Â NIR-CDsÂ can Â be Â categor- izedÂ into Â two Â stages Â based Â on Â chronological Â progres- sion.Â TheÂ firstÂ stage,Â knownÂ asÂ theÂ discoveryÂ andÂ pre- liminary Â development Â stage, Â spanned Â from Â 2007 Â to 2016.Â TheÂ secondÂ stage,Â referredÂ toÂ asÂ theÂ rapidÂ devel- opment Â stage, Â began Â in Â 2017 Â and Â continues Â to Â the present. Â In Â 2004, Â Xuâ€™sÂ team Â observed Â carbon Â nano- particlesÂ withÂ aÂ sizeÂ lessÂ thanÂ 10Â nmÂ duringÂ theÂ puri- ficationÂ processÂ ofÂ single-layerÂ carbonÂ nanotubes,Â and subsequentlyÂ discovered Â their Â unique Â optical Â proper- ties[157].Â InÂ 2006,Â theÂ Sunâ€™sÂ groupÂ usedÂ laserÂ ablationÂ of carbon Â targets Â to Â prepare Â similar Â fluorescent Â carbon nanoparticles, Â and Â first Â proposed Â the Â concept Â of CDs[158]. Â However, Â the Â research Â on Â CDs Â during Â this periodÂ wasÂ notÂ in-depth,Â andÂ thereÂ wereÂ fewÂ reportsÂ on NIR-CDs. InÂ the Â second Â stage, Â researchers Â began Â to Â con- duct Â a Â more Â in-depth Â exploration Â of Â the Â structureâ€“ activity Â relationship Â of Â NIR-CDs. Â The Â mainstream preparation Â strategies Â of Â NIR-CDs Â were Â developed successively Â in Â this Â period. Â In Â addition, Â the Â potential applicationsÂ ofÂ NIR-CDsÂ inÂ variousÂ fieldsÂ beganÂ toÂ re- ceiveÂ moreÂ andÂ moreÂ attention.Â ThisÂ stageÂ laidÂ aÂ solid foundation Â for Â the Â development Â of Â CD-basedÂ materi- als.Â AÂ seriesÂ ofÂ low-costÂ andÂ high-yieldÂ macro-prepar- ation Â strategies Â for Â NIR-CDs Â have Â been Â sequentially proposed. Â NIR-CDs, Â as Â biocompatible Â carbon-based nanomaterials, Â exhibit Â advantages Â such Â as Â excellent tissueÂ permeability,Â waterÂ solubility,Â andÂ lowÂ toxicity. TheseÂ characteristics Â make Â them Â promising Â candid- atesÂ for Â biomedical Â applications, Â serving Â as Â alternat- ivesÂ toÂ imagingÂ probes,Â photothermalÂ agents,Â photody- namicÂ agents,Â nanomaterials,Â andÂ organicÂ dyes.Â Com- pared Â to Â conventional Â visible Â light, Â NIR Â light Â offers deep Â penetration Â capabilities Â and Â a Â low Â interference background. Â As Â a Â result, Â NIR-CDsÂ have Â garnered Â in- creasingÂ attention Â due Â to Â their Â unique Â optical Â proper- ties.Â Moreover,Â NIR-CDsÂ withÂ highÂ photothermalÂ con- versionÂ efficiency Â are Â not Â only Â suitable Â for Â photo- thermalÂ imaging Â but Â can Â also Â generate Â detectable Â ul- trasound Â waves Â upon Â NIR Â laser Â irradiation, Â making themÂ applicable Â for Â deep Â tissue Â photoacoustic Â ima- gingÂ (PAI).Â PAI,Â dueÂ toÂ itsÂ excellentÂ spatialÂ resolution, canÂ provideÂ valuableÂ informationÂ acrossÂ nanometerÂ to micrometerÂ scalesÂ andÂ macroscopicÂ levels.Â Compared to Â other Â photothermal Â agents, Â NIR-CDs Â demonstrate good Â photostability, Â outstanding Â water Â solubility, straightforwardÂ preparation Â processes, Â andÂ absorption inÂ the Â NIRÂ region. Â These Â features Â maximize Â the Â con- versionÂ ofÂ absorbedÂ photonsÂ intoÂ heatÂ withinÂ physiolo- gical Â environments. Â Their Â small Â size Â facilitates Â renal clearance Â through Â metabolism, Â making Â NIR-CDsÂ ef- fectiveÂ agentsÂ forÂ PTT.Â CatalyticÂ therapyÂ basedÂ onÂ CD nanomaterialsÂ is Â predicated Â on Â the Â modulation Â of Â ox- idative Â stress Â within Â the Â tumor Â microenvironment. NanomaterialsÂ exhibitÂ inherentÂ enzyme-likeÂ character- istics,Â possessingÂ theÂ abilityÂ toÂ induceÂ changesÂ inÂ ROS levels Â and Â activate Â inflammasomes, Â leading Â to Â tumor cellÂ pyroptosis, Â ferroptosis, Â andÂ autophagy. Â ByÂ utiliz- ingÂ tunableÂ surfaceÂ chemistry,Â CDsÂ canÂ beÂ conjugated withÂ immunoadjuvants, Â photosensitizers, Â or Â chemo- therapeuticÂ agentsÂ forÂ useÂ inÂ immunotherapy,Â chemo- therapy,Â andÂ phototherapy,Â orÂ theirÂ combinations.Â Ad- ditionally,Â CDsÂ capableÂ ofÂ generatingÂ freeÂ radicalsÂ can be Â employed Â in Â photodynamic Â therapy[159]. Â However, current Â applications Â of Â NIR-CDsÂ are Â primarily Â fo- cused Â on Â oncology, Â and Â future Â research Â should Â also emphasize Â their Â potential Â in Â chronic Â diseases Â such Â as autoimmuneÂ disorders. ComparedÂ to Â the Â currently Â employed Â photolu- QianÂ HEÂ etÂ al:Â Near-infraredÂ carbonÂ dots:Â pioneeringÂ emergingÂ frontiersÂ inÂ biomedicalÂ applications Vol.Â 40Â |Â IssueÂ 1Â |Â Feb.Â 2025Â | 16 New Carbon Materials minescenceÂ materials,Â CDsÂ withÂ NIRÂ emissionÂ charac- teristics Â exhibit Â low Â toxicity, Â superior Â photostability, and Â targeting Â capabilities. Â These Â emerging Â NIR-CDs holdÂ significantÂ potentialÂ forÂ advancedÂ biomedicalÂ ap- plications. Â However, Â there Â are Â still Â some Â challenges: firstly, Â the Â controllable Â synthesis Â of Â near-infraredÂ ex- citationÂ orÂ emissionÂ CDsÂ isÂ notÂ yetÂ clear,Â andÂ thereÂ is currentlyÂ aÂ lackÂ ofÂ unifiedÂ standardsÂ forÂ synthesisÂ ma- terials Â andÂ methods[160]. Â Secondly, Â theÂ relativelyÂ short emission Â wavelengths Â of Â CDs Â are Â still Â the Â main obstacleÂ hinderingÂ theirÂ useÂ forÂ inÂ vivoÂ diseaseÂ monit- oringÂ andÂ image-guidedÂ surgeryÂ inÂ clinicalÂ transform- ation[4].Â The Â reason Â lies Â in Â not Â fully Â exploring Â the Â lu- minescence Â mechanism Â of Â NIR-CDs, Â and Â the strategiesÂ reportedÂ areÂ mostlyÂ empirical,Â makingÂ itÂ dif- ficult Â to Â guide Â the Â effective Â synthesis Â of Â long wavelength Â CDs[41]. Â Currently, Â four Â principal Â models areÂ widelyÂ endorsedÂ byÂ researchersÂ toÂ elucidateÂ theÂ lu- minescenceÂ mechanism Â of Â CDs: Â core Â state Â lumines- cence,Â surfaceÂ stateÂ luminescence,Â molecularÂ stateÂ lu- minescence, Â and Â cross-linking Â enhanced Â emission (CEE).Â AsÂ investigationsÂ intoÂ theÂ luminescenceÂ mech- anisms Â of Â CDs Â advance, Â it Â is Â becoming Â increasingly evident Â that Â the Â luminescent Â behavior Â arises Â from Â a complexÂ interplayÂ ofÂ multipleÂ factorsÂ ratherÂ thanÂ aÂ sin- gular, Â isolated Â mechanism. Â In Â the Â initial Â formation processÂ ofÂ CDs,Â smallÂ moleculesÂ undergoÂ polymeriza- tion, Â andÂ duringÂ this Â period, Â theÂ emissionÂ mechanism leans Â towards Â the Â molecular Â state. Â As Â the Â polymer structureÂ ofÂ CDsÂ forms,Â CEEÂ graduallyÂ becomesÂ dom- inant,Â andÂ withÂ theÂ increaseÂ inÂ carbonization,Â theÂ car- bon Â core Â gradually Â develops. Â The Â emergence Â of Â the carbon Â core Â signifies Â the Â appearance Â of Â a Â core-shell structure.Â AtÂ theÂ sameÂ time,Â theÂ fluorescenceÂ emission mechanism Â transitions Â to Â the Â carbon Â core, Â which Â is jointlyÂ determinedÂ byÂ sizeÂ andÂ surfaceÂ state. Â TheÂ sur- faceÂ stateÂ isÂ notÂ determinedÂ byÂ isolatedÂ sideÂ chainsÂ or functional Â groups, Â but Â rather Â by Â the Â hybridization Â of the Â carbon Â backbone Â and Â the Â connected Â chemical groups. Â When Â near Â complete Â carbonization Â occurs, surface Â functional Â groups Â and Â side Â chains Â disappear, and Â the Â size Â effect Â become Â dominant. Â Currently, Â the fluorescence Â mechanism Â of Â CDs Â with Â a Â core-shell structure Â is Â the Â most Â extensively Â studied. Â Therefore, theÂ fluorescenceÂ emissionÂ ofÂ CDsÂ canÂ beÂ controlledÂ by adjustingÂ theÂ coreÂ andÂ surfaceÂ functionalÂ groups.Â Low fluorescence Â quantum Â efficiency Â is Â another Â major drawbackÂ ofÂ NIR-CDs,Â especiallyÂ inÂ casesÂ ofÂ exceed- ingÂ 800Â nmÂ and/orÂ beingÂ stableÂ inÂ water,Â whichÂ isÂ still rare,Â greatlyÂ limitingÂ theirÂ applicationÂ inÂ organisms.Â If NIR-CDs Â are Â used Â for Â sensing, Â imaging, Â and Â therapy inÂ organisms, Â they Â also Â require Â tedious Â surface Â func- tionalizationÂ procedures. Â Simultaneously, Â it Â is Â imper- ativeÂ toÂ conductÂ comprehensiveÂ researchÂ onÂ theÂ meta- bolicÂ behaviorÂ andÂ biologicalÂ safetyÂ ofÂ CDsÂ withinÂ liv- ingÂ organisms.Â CDsÂ mayÂ encounterÂ variousÂ biological barriers, Â immune Â responses, Â and Â limited Â circulation duration. Â Therefore, Â there Â is Â still Â a Â significant Â way ahead Â for Â the Â future Â development Â and Â application Â of CDs, Â especially Â NIR-â…¡Â CDs.Â Through Â the Â above Â ef- forts,Â theÂ vigorousÂ developmentÂ ofÂ NIR-CDsÂ willÂ pro- moteÂ theirÂ applicationsÂ inÂ biosensing,Â bioimaging,Â and therapy. Â Meanwhile, Â it Â also Â opens Â up Â new Â pathways for Â designing Â and Â synthesizing Â biocompatible Â CDs thatÂ meetÂ theÂ demandsÂ ofÂ clinicalÂ applications.Â Declaration of competing interest TheÂ authorsÂ declareÂ noÂ conflictÂ ofÂ interest. Â Carbon Â dots Â with Â absorption Â red- shifting Â for Â two-photon Â fluorescence Â imaging Â of Â tumor Â tissue Â p

item-info.ce:copyright: Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

head.ce:title: Near-infrared carbon dots: pioneering emerging frontiers in biomedical applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Third Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, Shanxi Bethune Hospital, Shanxi Academy of Medical Sciences, Tongji Shanxi Hospital, Taiyuan 030032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Third Hospital of Shanxi Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Third Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, Shanxi Bethune Hospital, Shanxi Academy of Medical Sciences, Tongji Shanxi Hospital, Taiyuan 030032, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Qian HE and Yan-li YANG contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials with sizes smaller than 10 nm, that are renowned for their exceptional properties, including superior antiphotobleaching, excellent biocompatibility, and minimal toxicity, which have received significant interest. Near-infrared (NIR) light has emerged as an ideal light source in the biological field due to its advantages of minimal scattering and absorption, long wavelength emission, increased tissue penetration, and reduced interference from biological backgrounds. CDs with efficient absorption and/or emission characteristics in the NIR spectrum have shown remarkable promise in the biomedical uses. This study provides a comprehensive overview of the preparation methods and wavelength modulation strategies for near-infrared CDs and reviews research progress in their use in the areas of biosensing, bioimaging, and therapy. It also discusses current challenges and clinical prospects, aimed at deepening our understanding of the subject and promoting further advances in this field.
